user: A 58-year-old male with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents to the emergency department with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to his left arm for the past 90 minutes. His ECG shows 3 mm ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL. Troponin I is 4.2 ng/mL (normal <0.04 ng/mL). What is the diagnosis and the territory of infarction?
ground_truth_answer: ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) of the inferior wall. ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal changes in I and aVL localizes the infarction to the inferior wall of the left ventricle, which is supplied by the right coronary artery (RCA) in the majority of patients. The markedly elevated troponin I confirms myocardial necrosis.

user: A 68-year-old woman with a history of rheumatic fever presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and an opening snap followed by a low-pitched diastolic rumble best heard at the cardiac apex with the bell of the stethoscope. Echocardiography reveals a mitral valve area of 1.2 cm² (normal 4–6 cm²) with a mean gradient of 10 mmHg across the mitral valve. What is the diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Severe mitral stenosis. A mitral valve area of 1.2 cm² meets the echocardiographic criterion for severe mitral stenosis (MVA ≤1.5 cm²), and a mean gradient of 10 mmHg further corroborates hemodynamically significant obstruction. The opening snap and diastolic rumble at the apex are classic auscultatory findings of mitral stenosis, most commonly caused by prior rheumatic fever.

user: A 45-year-old man with no significant past medical history is found on a routine ECG to have a PR interval of 320 ms, and each P wave is followed by a QRS complex with no dropped beats. The QRS duration is 88 ms and there are no other abnormalities. What is the ECG diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: First-degree atrioventricular (AV) block. A PR interval greater than 200 ms (0.20 seconds) with every P wave conducting to a QRS complex and no dropped beats is the definition of first-degree AV block. The PR interval of 320 ms clearly exceeds this threshold. This is a benign finding requiring no immediate intervention in an otherwise healthy individual.

user: A 74-year-old man with a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy presents for a follow-up visit. His most recent echocardiogram shows a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 32%. He is currently on optimal medical therapy including lisinopril 10 mg daily and carvedilol 25 mg twice daily. His QRS duration on ECG is 158 ms with a left bundle branch block (LBBB) morphology and he remains in NYHA Class III heart failure despite therapy. Which device therapy is indicated?
ground_truth_answer: Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT), either as a CRT-D (defibrillator) or CRT-P (pacemaker). This patient meets all guideline indications for CRT: LVEF ≤35%, QRS duration ≥150 ms with LBBB morphology, NYHA Class III symptoms, and optimized medical therapy. CRT improves ventricular synchrony, reduces symptoms, and decreases mortality in this population. Given his ischemic etiology and reduced EF, a CRT-D is typically preferred to also address sudden cardiac death risk.

user: A 22-year-old competitive swimmer collapses during practice. He is resuscitated and brought to the emergency department. His ECG shows deep T-wave inversions in leads V1–V4, and echocardiography demonstrates asymmetric septal hypertrophy with an interventricular septal thickness of 22 mm (normal ≤11 mm) and a systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve. Left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) gradient is 45 mmHg at rest. What is the diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM). The interventricular septal thickness of 22 mm far exceeds the diagnostic threshold of ≥15 mm for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and the presence of systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve with a resting LVOT gradient of 45 mmHg (≥30 mmHg is significant) confirms the obstructive phenotype. Deep T-wave inversions in the precordial leads are characteristic. Sudden cardiac death in young athletes is a classic presentation of previously undiagnosed HOCM.

user: A 45-year-old woman presents for evaluation of fatigue, weight gain of 12 lbs over 6 months, cold intolerance, constipation, and dry skin. Labs reveal TSH 18.4 mIU/L (reference 0.4–4.0 mIU/L) and free T4 0.5 ng/dL (reference 0.8–1.8 ng/dL). What is the diagnosis and first-line treatment?
ground_truth_answer: Primary hypothyroidism. The elevated TSH (18.4 mIU/L) indicates the pituitary is sensing insufficient thyroid hormone, and the low free T4 (0.5 ng/dL) confirms reduced thyroid output. This pattern — high TSH with low free T4 — defines primary hypothyroidism. First-line treatment is levothyroxine (synthetic T4), dosed at approximately 1.6 mcg/kg/day orally, with TSH rechecked in 6–8 weeks to titrate the dose.

user: A 32-year-old man with type 1 diabetes is found unresponsive by his roommate. Point-of-care glucose reads 28 mg/dL. He is unable to swallow safely. No IV access is immediately available. What is the appropriate immediate intervention?
ground_truth_answer: Administer intramuscular (IM) or subcutaneous glucagon (1 mg for adults). Because the patient is unconscious and cannot swallow safely, oral glucose is contraindicated due to aspiration risk, and IV dextrose is not immediately available. Glucagon stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis, rapidly raising blood glucose. After he regains consciousness, oral carbohydrates should be given and the cause of hypoglycemia investigated.

user: A 28-year-old woman is referred for secondary amenorrhea of 8 months duration. She reports galactorrhea and occasional headaches. Serum prolactin is 142 ng/mL (reference < 25 ng/mL), TSH is 1.8 mIU/L, and a pituitary MRI shows a 12 mm enhancing sellar mass. What is the diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Prolactinoma (macroprolactinoma). The markedly elevated prolactin (142 ng/mL), galactorrhea, secondary amenorrhea (due to prolactin-mediated suppression of GnRH pulsatility), and a pituitary adenoma measuring 12 mm (>10 mm = macroadenoma) on MRI together confirm this diagnosis. Normal TSH rules out hypothyroidism as a secondary cause of elevated prolactin. First-line treatment is a dopamine agonist such as cabergoline.

user: A 55-year-old man with hypertension refractory to three antihypertensives is found to have serum potassium of 2.9 mEq/L (reference 3.5–5.0 mEq/L) on routine labs despite not taking diuretics. Plasma aldosterone concentration is 38 ng/dL and plasma renin activity is 0.3 ng/mL/hr, yielding an aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) of 127 (reference < 30). What is the most likely diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome). The triad of refractory hypertension, spontaneous hypokalemia (without diuretic use), and a markedly elevated aldosterone-to-renin ratio of 127 (well above the diagnostic threshold of 30) confirms autonomous aldosterone excess with suppressed renin — the hallmark of primary hyperaldosteronism. The next step is confirmatory testing (e.g., oral sodium loading or fludrocortisone suppression test) followed by adrenal CT to identify a unilateral adenoma versus bilateral hyperplasia.

user: A 38-year-old woman presents with a 2-cm solitary thyroid nodule discovered incidentally on neck ultrasound. Ultrasound characterizes it as solid, hypoechoic, with irregular margins and microcalcifications — features assigned ACR TI-RADS category 5. Her TSH is 1.1 mIU/L. What is the recommended next step in management?
ground_truth_answer: Fine-needle aspiration biopsy (FNA) of the nodule. ACR TI-RADS category 5 nodules carry high suspicion for malignancy based on their ultrasonographic features (solid, hypoechoic, irregular margins, microcalcifications). Per ACR TI-RADS guidelines, FNA is recommended for TR5 nodules ≥1 cm, and this nodule at 2 cm clearly meets that threshold. Normal TSH (1.1 mIU/L) makes a hyperfunctioning (benign) nodule unlikely, reinforcing the need for cytologic evaluation.

user: A 28-year-old woman with no prior psychiatric history presents after her third emergency department visit this month for chest pain, palpitations, and shortness of breath. Each episode lasts 8-12 minutes, peaks within 4 minutes, and resolves spontaneously. Cardiac workup including ECG, troponin, and Holter monitor are all normal. She reports intense fear of dying during episodes and now avoids driving and crowded malls because she fears having an episode in public. What is the most likely diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Panic disorder with agoraphobia. The discrete episodes with rapid peak onset (within 10 minutes), multiple somatic symptoms (chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea), fear of dying, and complete negative cardiac workup are classic for panic attacks. The subsequent avoidance of driving and crowded places to prevent future attacks meets DSM-5 criteria for agoraphobia as a comorbid specifier.

user: A 34-year-old man with a known diagnosis of bipolar I disorder presents to clinic. His lithium level drawn this morning is 1.8 mEq/L (therapeutic range 0.6-1.2 mEq/L for maintenance). He reports coarse hand tremor, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea that began two days ago. On examination he is confused and has ataxia. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
ground_truth_answer: Lithium toxicity requiring immediate discontinuation of lithium and emergent medical evaluation for hemodialysis. A lithium level of 1.8 mEq/L combined with neurological symptoms (confusion, ataxia, coarse tremor) and gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) confirms moderate-to-severe lithium toxicity. Lithium must be stopped immediately, IV fluids initiated to promote renal clearance, and nephrology consulted for hemodialysis given the neurological involvement.

user: During a routine follow-up, a 45-year-old man on haloperidol 10 mg/day for schizophrenia develops sudden-onset sustained upward deviation of both eyes with neck extension and inability to bring his gaze to the midline. The symptom began approximately 90 minutes after his morning dose. He is alert and distressed. Vital signs are stable. What is the diagnosis and appropriate treatment?
ground_truth_answer: Acute oculogyric crisis, a form of acute dystonia secondary to haloperidol (a typical antipsychotic with high D2 receptor blockade). The sudden onset of sustained involuntary upward gaze deviation and neck extension temporally linked to dopamine antagonist administration is diagnostic. Treatment is immediate administration of an anticholinergic agent such as benztropine 1-2 mg IM or IV, or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IM or IV, which produces rapid symptom resolution.

user: A 52-year-old woman presents reporting at least 6 months of persistent worry about her finances, her adult children's health, and her job performance, which she describes as difficult to control. She endorses fatigue, difficulty concentrating, muscle tension, irritability, and sleep-onset insomnia occurring more days than not. She denies discrete panic attacks, obsessions, compulsions, and her symptoms are not attributable to substance use or a medical condition. PHQ-9 score is 4. What is the diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD). DSM-5 criteria are fully met: excessive anxiety and worry about multiple domains (finances, health, work) for more than 6 months, difficulty controlling the worry, and the presence of at least 3 associated symptoms (fatigue, concentration difficulty, muscle tension, irritability, insomnia). Symptoms cause functional impairment and are not better explained by another mental disorder, substance use, or medical condition. The low PHQ-9 score argues against a primary depressive disorder.

user: A 23-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after being found running in traffic and shouting that he is on a divine mission to save the world. His girlfriend reports he has not slept in 4 days, has been spending thousands of dollars on cryptocurrency, and has been making rapid, non-stop speeches. He has no prior psychiatric history and urine toxicology is negative for all substances. Mental status exam reveals pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions, and markedly elevated mood. What is the most likely diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Bipolar I Disorder, current episode manic with psychotic features. The patient meets full DSM-5 criteria for a manic episode: elevated mood lasting more than 7 days with severely impaired functioning requiring emergency intervention, plus at least 3 'DIGFAST' criteria (Distractibility, decreased need for sleep with only 4 days without sleep, Grandiosity with divine mission beliefs, Flight of ideas, Activity/psychomotor agitation, Speech pressured, Thoughtless spending). Grandiose delusions confirm psychotic features. Negative toxicology excludes substance-induced mania.

user: A 58-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of bilateral hand stiffness lasting more than 1 hour each morning, symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, and fatigue. Laboratory results show RF positive at 120 IU/mL (reference <14), anti-CCP antibody positive at 250 U/mL (reference <17), ESR 68 mm/hr, and CRP 3.2 mg/dL. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia but no erosions. What is the diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Rheumatoid arthritis. The patient meets ACR/EULAR 2010 criteria with a score well above the threshold of 6: large number of small joints involved (>3 small joints, score 3), strongly positive RF and anti-CCP both above 3x upper limit of normal (score 3), elevated acute phase reactants ESR and CRP (score 1), and symptom duration >6 weeks (score 1), totaling at least 8 points. The combination of symmetric small joint synovitis, prolonged morning stiffness, high-titer seropositivity for both RF and anti-CCP, and elevated inflammatory markers confirms rheumatoid arthritis.

user: A 45-year-old man with a known history of hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe pain, erythema, and swelling of his right first metatarsophalangeal joint that began overnight. The joint is exquisitely tender to palpation. Synovial fluid analysis reveals a WBC of 28,000 cells/µL with 90% neutrophils, and polarized light microscopy demonstrates negatively birefringent, needle-shaped crystals. Serum uric acid is 9.8 mg/dL. What is the diagnosis and what medication contributed to this presentation?
ground_truth_answer: Acute gouty arthritis (podagra) of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint, precipitated in part by hydrochlorothiazide. The diagnosis is confirmed by the pathognomonic finding of negatively birefringent, needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals on polarized light microscopy, combined with the classic clinical presentation of acute monoarthritis at the first MTP joint. Hydrochlorothiazide is a thiazide diuretic that reduces renal uric acid excretion, leading to hyperuricemia (serum uric acid 9.8 mg/dL), which predisposes to urate crystal deposition and acute gout flares.

Laboratory evaluation shows ANA positive at 1:640 with a homogeneous pattern, anti-dsDNA antibody 185 IU/mL (reference <30), anti-Smith antibody positive, complement C3 52 mg/dL (reference 90-180) and C4 8 mg/dL (reference 16-47), CBC showing WBC 3,200 cells/µL, hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL, and platelets 95,000/µL. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein and RBC casts. What is the diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). The patient satisfies the 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria for SLE with a score far exceeding the threshold of 10: malar rash, oral ulcers, pleuritis, arthritis, leukopenia (WBC <4,000), hemolytic or inflammatory anemia, thrombocytopenia (<100,000), proteinuria with RBC casts indicating lupus nephritis, positive ANA at ≥1:80, high-titer anti-dsDNA, positive anti-Smith (a highly specific antibody for SLE), and low complement levels (C3 and C4), all together confirming SLE with active renal involvement.

user: A 68-year-old woman presents with a 4-month history of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle aching and stiffness, worse in the mornings and lasting about 90 minutes. She also reports new-onset headache over her right temple, jaw claudication while chewing, and tenderness on palpation of the right temporal artery. ESR is 95 mm/hr and CRP is 6.8 mg/dL. She denies visual changes currently but reports one episode of transient blurring of vision in the right eye last week. What is the diagnosis and what is the immediate management priority?
ground_truth_answer: Giant cell arteritis (GCA) with concurrent polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) and impending ischemic complication. The diagnosis of GCA is supported by age >50, new headache, jaw claudication, temporal artery tenderness, markedly elevated ESR (>50 mm/hr) and CRP, and a prior episode of transient visual loss indicating threatened ischemia to the ophthalmic artery. The concurrent bilateral shoulder and hip girdle stiffness with prolonged morning stiffness represents PMR, which occurs in 40-60% of GCA cases. The immediate management priority is high-dose corticosteroid therapy — intravenous methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg daily for 3 days given the recent visual symptom — to prevent irreversible bilateral blindness, followed by temporal artery biopsy to confirm the diagnosis, which should not delay steroid initiation.

user: A 28-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of lower back pain and stiffness that is worse in the morning and improves with exercise but not with rest. He also reports pain and swelling in his right Achilles tendon insertion. HLA-B27 antigen is positive. Pelvic X-ray shows bilateral grade 2 sacroiliitis with blurring of the sacroiliac joint margins and small areas of sclerosis. MRI of the sacroiliac joints confirms bilateral bone marrow edema. CRP is 2.1 mg/dL. What is the diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Ankylosing spondylitis (AS), classified under axial spondyloarthritis. The diagnosis is established by the modified New York criteria: chronic low back pain with inflammatory characteristics (age of onset <45, improvement with exercise but not rest, morning stiffness), bilateral radiographic sacroiliitis of at least grade 2, positive HLA-B27 (present in ~90% of AS patients), and enthesitis at the Achilles tendon insertion — a hallmark feature of spondyloarthropathies. MRI confirmation of sacroiliac joint bone marrow edema corroborates active inflammation. The combination satisfies both clinical and imaging criteria for definite ankylosing spondylitis.

ground_truth_answer: IV alteplase (tPA) at 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg, 10% as bolus then 90% over 60 minutes) followed immediately by mechanical thrombectomy (endovascular therapy). The patient is within the 4.5-hour thrombolysis window with no contraindications (INR 1.0, platelets normal, glucose normal, no hemorrhage on imaging, BP controllable below 185/110). The confirmed M1 occlusion with NIHSS ≥6 and large territory involvement meets criteria for mechanical thrombectomy per AHA/ASA guidelines (DAWN, DEFUSE-3, MR CLEAN trial criteria), and bridging IV tPA before thrombectomy is standard of care for eligible patients at a thrombectomy-capable center.

user: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of episodic unilateral throbbing headaches lasting 6–18 hours, accompanied by nausea, phonophobia, and photophobia, occurring approximately 4 days per month. She has been taking ibuprofen 800 mg almost daily for the past 3 months. Over the past 6 weeks, her headaches have increased to 22 days per month and are now bilateral, dull, and present upon waking. She denies fever, neck stiffness, visual changes, or focal neurological deficits. Neurological exam is normal. What is the most accurate diagnosis explaining the change in her headache pattern?
ground_truth_answer: Medication overuse headache (MOH), also known as analgesic rebound headache, superimposed on a pre-existing episodic migraine disorder. The diagnostic criteria are met: she has a pre-existing primary headache disorder (episodic migraine), she has been using a simple analgesic (ibuprofen/NSAID) on ≥15 days per month for >3 months, and her headache has transformed to ≥15 days per month with a changed character (now bilateral, dull, present on waking). Per ICHD-3 criteria, NSAIDs used ≥15 days/month qualify as the overused medication. The transformation from unilateral throbbing to daily bilateral dull headache upon analgesic overuse is pathognomonic for MOH. Treatment requires supervised analgesic withdrawal and initiation of preventive migraine therapy.

user: A 52-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history is evaluated for progressive proximal muscle weakness of his lower extremities over 4 months, difficulty climbing stairs, and areflexia. He reports that his strength paradoxically improves transiently after repeated use of his limbs. EMG shows a low-amplitude compound muscle action potential (CMAP) at rest that markedly increases (>100% facilitation) with high-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation at 50 Hz. Serum calcium is 11.8 mg/dL. Chest CT reveals a 3.2 cm right hilar mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy. What is the unifying diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS) as a paraneoplastic syndrome secondary to small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC). The clinical triad of proximal muscle weakness, hyporeflexia, and autonomic dysfunction combined with the pathognomonic EMG finding of >100% CMAP increment with high-frequency (50 Hz) repetitive stimulation confirms LEMS. Unlike myasthenia gravis, LEMS shows facilitating (incrementing) rather than decrementing response, and strength improves transiently with repeated muscle use. The 30-pack-year smoking history, hilar mass with mediastinal adenopathy, and hypercalcemia (from ectopic PTHrP production by SCLC) all point to SCLC as the underlying malignancy. LEMS in this context is mediated by autoantibodies against presynaptic P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channels, impeding acetylcholine vesicle release.

user: A 28-year-old woman presents after her second witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure in 6 months, both occurring within 1–2 hours of waking. She also reports brief episodes since adolescence of bilateral hand jerking while eating breakfast, occasionally causing her to drop utensils, which she had attributed to 'clumsiness.' EEG shows generalized 3–6 Hz polyspike-and-wave discharges most prominent in the morning. MRI brain is normal. She takes no medications. What is the diagnosis and most appropriate first-line antiepileptic drug?
ground_truth_answer: Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy (JME). The diagnostic criteria are fulfilled: onset in adolescence, myoclonic jerks predominantly in the morning after waking (previously unrecognized), generalized tonic-clonic seizures occurring upon awakening, and generalized polyspike-and-wave discharges on EEG with a normal MRI indicating a primary generalized epilepsy syndrome. JME is one of the most common idiopathic generalized epilepsies. The first-line antiepileptic drug is valproic acid (valproate sodium), which is highly effective across all three seizure types seen in JME (myoclonic, generalized tonic-clonic, and absence). In women of childbearing age where valproate is relatively contraindicated due to teratogenicity, levetiracetam is the preferred alternative, but based on the information given without explicit contraindication documentation, valproate remains the evidence-based first-line agent per ILAE guidelines for JME.

user: A 71-year-old man presents with a 9-month history of progressive resting tremor of his right hand, cogwheel rigidity in bilateral upper extremities, bradykinesia, and a shuffling festinating gait with reduced arm swing. He has no history of neuroleptic exposure, no cerebellar signs, no early falls, no vertical gaze palsy, no significant autonomic dysfunction, and no hallucinations. Cognitive testing is normal. Brain MRI shows subtle T2 hypointensity in the posterolateral putamen bilaterally. DaTscan (ioflupane I-123 SPECT) demonstrates markedly reduced dopamine transporter uptake in the posterior putamen bilaterally with relative sparing of the caudate, producing a 'period without a dot' or comma-shaped pattern. What is the diagnosis?
ground_truth_answer: Idiopathic Parkinson's Disease (IPD). The clinical picture meets the MDS (Movement Disorder Society) criteria for clinically established Parkinson's disease: bradykinesia plus at least one of resting tremor or rigidity, with absence of absolute exclusion criteria (no neuroleptic exposure, no cerebellar signs, no vertical gaze palsy, no early postural instability/falls) and presence of supportive criteria. The DaTscan showing asymmetric reduced uptake in the posterior putamen with relative caudate sparing ('comma' or 'period without a dot' pattern) is the imaging hallmark of nigrostriatal dopaminergic degeneration, confirming presynaptic dopaminergic deficit consistent with IPD. The T2 hypointensity in the posterolateral putamen on MRI reflects iron deposition, an additional supportive finding in PD. The absence of atypical features (early autonomic failure, vertical gaze palsy, rapid progression) excludes Parkinson-plus syndromes such as MSA or PSP.

user: A 28-year-old male traveler returns from rural sub-Saharan Africa after a 3-week safari. He presents 10 days after return with fever (39.8°C), rigors, headache, and myalgias. Peripheral blood smear shows intraerythrocytic ring-form trophozoites with multiple rings per erythrocyte, banana-shaped gametocytes, and no visible hemozoin in monocytes. Parasitemia is 6.5%. Rapid diagnostic test is positive for HRP-2 antigen. He took atovaquone-proguanil prophylaxis but admits to missing several doses. What is the causative organism and what first-line regimen is indicated?
ground_truth_answer: Plasmodium falciparum malaria. The diagnostic features — multiple ring forms per erythrocyte, banana-shaped (crescentic) gametocytes, HRP-2 positivity, and absence of hemozoin in monocytes — are pathognomonic for P. falciparum. Parasitemia at 6.5% indicates severe or high-density infection. Because the patient was taking atovaquone-proguanil (which failed due to non-adherence), and given P. falciparum's resistance profile in sub-Saharan Africa, first-line treatment for uncomplicated high-density falciparum malaria in a non-immune traveler is IV artesunate (preferred over quinine for severe/high parasitemia), transitioning to oral artemether-lumefantrine upon clinical improvement. If parasitemia were below 1% and the patient were hemodynamically stable, oral artemether-lumefantrine alone would suffice; the 6.5% parasitemia threshold mandates parenteral artesunate.

user: A 45-year-old HIV-positive male (CD4 count 38 cells/µL, HIV RNA 310,000 copies/mL, not on antiretroviral therapy) presents with 3 weeks of progressive dyspnea on exertion, non-productive cough, and low-grade fever (38.1°C). Chest X-ray shows bilateral diffuse interstitial infiltrates in a 'ground-glass' pattern. ABG on room air: pH 7.44, PaO2 52 mmHg, PaCO2 31 mmHg, A-a gradient 58 mmHg. LDH is 620 IU/L. Bronchoscopic BAL with Grocott methenamine silver stain reveals disc-shaped, cup-like cysts 4–6 µm in diameter. What is the diagnosis and what treatment should be initiated, including adjunctive therapy given the ABG?
ground_truth_answer: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP). The clinical constellation — CD4 count below 200 cells/µL (here 38), bilateral ground-glass infiltrates, elevated LDH (620 IU/L), PaO2 of 52 mmHg (indicating moderate-to-severe disease by the criterion of PaO2 <70 mmHg on room air), and GMS stain demonstrating characteristic disc-shaped cysts — is diagnostic of PCP. First-line treatment is trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) at 15–20 mg/kg/day of the TMP component IV or orally in 3–4 divided doses for 21 days. Because PaO2 is <70 mmHg (or A-a gradient >35 mmHg), adjunctive corticosteroids are mandated: prednisone 40 mg twice daily for 5 days, then 40 mg daily for 5 days, then 20 mg daily until completion of antimicrobial therapy. ART should be initiated within 2 weeks of PCP treatment to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) while still improving survival.

user: A 62-year-old male with a bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement performed 14 months ago presents with 6 weeks of low-grade fever, fatigue, and a new regurgitant murmur. Blood cultures drawn on three separate occasions over 48 hours each yield Gram-positive cocci in clusters that are coagulase-positive, catalase-positive, and oxacillin MIC is 0.5 µg/mL (susceptible by CLSI breakpoint of ≤2 µg/mL for Staphylococcus aureus). Transesophageal echocardiogram shows a 12 mm oscillating vegetation on the prosthetic valve leaflet with partial dehiscence. What is the causative organism and what is the definitive antimicrobial regimen?
ground_truth_answer: Methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) prosthetic valve endocarditis. Coagulase-positive, catalase-positive Gram-positive cocci are S. aureus; oxacillin MIC of 0.5 µg/mL is below the CLSI susceptibility breakpoint of ≤2 µg/mL, confirming MSSA (not MRSA). For MSSA prosthetic valve endocarditis, the definitive regimen per AHA/IDSA guidelines is nafcillin or oxacillin 2 g IV every 4 hours (anti-staphylococcal penicillin) for a minimum of 6 weeks, combined with rifampin 300 mg orally every 8 hours for the entire 6 weeks, plus gentamicin 1 mg/kg IV every 8 hours for the first 2 weeks only. The vegetation exceeding 10 mm with valve dehiscence also meets surgical indications (Class I recommendation), so cardiothoracic surgery consultation for valve replacement is required. Vancomycin is reserved for penicillin allergy and is inferior for MSSA.

user: A 34-year-old pregnant woman at 32 weeks gestation presents with fever (38.9°C), right flank pain, dysuria, and costovertebral angle tenderness. Urinalysis shows 3+ leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, WBC >50/hpf, and bacteriuria. Urine culture grows >100,000 CFU/mL of a Gram-negative rod that is oxidase-negative, lactose-fermenting, indole-positive, and produces a positive methyl red test. Antibiotic susceptibilities: ampicillin resistant (MIC >16 µg/mL), ceftriaxone susceptible (MIC 0.5 µg/mL), ciprofloxacin susceptible but contraindicated in pregnancy. What is the causative organism and what is the appropriate treatment?
ground_truth_answer: Escherichia coli pyelonephritis in pregnancy. The biochemical profile — oxidase-negative, lactose-fermenting, indole-positive, positive methyl red test — is characteristic of E. coli within the Enterobacteriaceae. Ampicillin resistance (MIC >16 µg/mL) is common in E. coli due to beta-lactamase production. Fluoroquinolones are contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic risk, fetal cartilage toxicity). The appropriate treatment for uncomplicated E. coli pyelonephritis in pregnancy with a ceftriaxone-susceptible isolate is IV ceftriaxone 1–2 g every 24 hours until afebrile for 48 hours, then step-down to oral cephalexin or cefpodoxime to complete a total 10–14 day course. Hospitalization is required because pyelonephritis in pregnancy carries significant risks including preterm labor, urosepsis, and acute respiratory distress syndrome. Repeat urine culture 1–2 weeks after completion is mandatory to confirm eradication.

user: A 55-year-old male with a 20 pack-year smoking history and type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.8%) presents in December with 5 days of fever (39.2°C), rigors, productive cough with rust-colored sputum, pleuritic chest pain, and confusion (MMSE 18/30). Vital signs: BP 88/54 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, RR 28/min, SpO2 88% on room air. CXR demonstrates a dense right lower lobe lobar consolidation. BUN 28 mg/dL, serum sodium 129 mEq/L. Sputum Gram stain shows Gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci in pairs. Blood cultures and urine pneumococcal antigen are pending. What is the CURB-65 score, the corresponding mortality risk category, and the recommended site and regimen of care?
ground_truth_answer: Streptococcus pneumoniae community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) with a CURB-65 score of 4. CURB-65 components: Confusion (MMSE <24) = 1 point; Urea/BUN >19 mg/dL (BUN 28) = 1 point; Respiratory rate ≥30/min (RR 28 is just below; however, the criterion is ≥30, so this scores 0) — correcting: RR is 28, which does not meet the ≥30 threshold, so RR = 0; Blood pressure <90 systolic or ≤60 diastolic (BP 88/54) = 1 point; Age ≥65 = 0 (patient is 55). Total CURB-65 = 3. A score of 3 corresponds to severe CAP with predicted 30-day mortality of approximately 17%, mandating hospitalization, with consideration of ICU admission given hemodynamic instability (BP 88/54 mmHg meets septic shock criteria). Gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci confirm presumptive S. pneumoniae. Per IDSA/ATS guidelines, severe CAP requiring ICU-level care is treated with beta-lactam (ceftriaxone 1–2 g IV every 24 hours) plus a respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin 750 mg IV daily) or an advanced macrolide; given ICU admission, the combination of ceftriaxone plus azithromycin IV is also guideline-concordant. Empiric coverage for MRSA (vancomycin or linezolid) is not indicated without specific risk factors. Supplemental oxygen and vasopressor support for septic shock are required.

Labs reveal serum creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL), BUN 28 mg/dL, serum albumin 2.1 g/dL, total cholesterol 310 mg/dL, and urinalysis showing 4+ proteinuria. A 24-hour urine collection demonstrates 5.8 g of protein. Serum complement C3 is 42 mg/dL (normal 90–180) and C4 is 6 mg/dL (normal 16–47), and anti-dsDNA antibodies are markedly elevated at 1:640. Kidney biopsy reveals diffuse endocapillary proliferation with subendothelial immune deposits on electron microscopy and a full-house immunofluorescence pattern (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q). What is the diagnosis and its ISN/RPS classification?
ground_truth_answer: The diagnosis is lupus nephritis, ISN/RPS Class IV (diffuse lupus nephritis). The clinical triad of nephrotic-range proteinuria (>3.5 g/day), hypocomplementemia (low C3 and C4), and markedly elevated anti-dsDNA in an SLE patient strongly suggests active lupus nephritis. The kidney biopsy finding of diffuse endocapillary proliferation (affecting >50% of glomeruli) with subendothelial immune complex deposits and full-house immunofluorescence pattern is pathognomonic for Class IV diffuse lupus nephritis per the ISN/RPS 2003 classification. This is the most severe and common form of lupus nephritis requiring aggressive immunosuppression with mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide plus high-dose corticosteroids.

user: A 62-year-old male with stage 3b chronic kidney disease (eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73m²), type 2 diabetes, and hypertension presents for routine follow-up. Current medications include metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, and amlodipine 5 mg daily. His most recent labs show serum potassium 5.6 mEq/L, bicarbonate 19 mEq/L, phosphorus 5.2 mg/dL, PTH 142 pg/mL (normal 15–65), 25-OH vitamin D 14 ng/mL, and hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL with a reticulocyte count of 1.1% and transferrin saturation of 32% with ferritin 210 ng/mL. His urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio is 620 mg/g. Based on KDIGO guidelines, which medication in his current regimen requires immediate discontinuation and why?
ground_truth_answer: Metformin must be immediately discontinued. Per KDIGO and FDA guidelines, metformin is contraindicated when eGFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73m², and its use should be reviewed and typically stopped when eGFR drops below 45 mL/min/1.73m². This patient's eGFR of 32 mL/min/1.73m² places him at high risk for metformin accumulation due to impaired renal clearance, which can precipitate life-threatening lactic acidosis. The drug's elimination is almost entirely renal, and accumulation at this level of kidney function represents an unacceptable risk. Lisinopril is appropriate (it reduces proteinuria and is indicated in diabetic CKD) and amlodipine is safe, but metformin must be stopped immediately and replaced with a safer glucose-lowering agent such as a DPP-4 inhibitor (with dose adjustment) or insulin.

user: A 45-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe flank pain radiating to the groin, nausea, and hematuria. A non-contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis reveals a 6 mm hyperdense calculus in the right ureter at the ureterovesical junction, with moderate right-sided hydronephrosis. Urine pH is 5.0, and 24-hour urine chemistries show urine uric acid 980 mg/day (normal <800), uric acid/creatinine ratio 0.9, and serum uric acid of 9.8 mg/dL. Urine calcium is 145 mg/day (normal <250), oxalate 22 mg/day (normal <40), and citrate 680 mg/day (normal >320). Stone analysis from a prior episode shows 100% uric acid composition. What is the most appropriate long-term medical preventive therapy for this patient's nephrolithiasis?
ground_truth_answer: The most appropriate long-term preventive therapy is urinary alkalinization with potassium citrate combined with allopurinol. This patient has pure uric acid nephrolithiasis driven by hyperuricosuria (uric acid excretion >800 mg/day) and hyperuricemia (serum uric acid 9.8 mg/dL), with a persistently acidic urine pH of 5.0. Uric acid stones form specifically in acidic urine (pH <5.5) because uric acid is poorly soluble at low pH. Potassium citrate alkalinizes the urine to a target pH of 6.0–6.5, dramatically increasing uric acid solubility and preventing stone formation; it also provides citrate, which is already adequate in this patient. Allopurinol, a xanthine oxidase inhibitor, reduces uric acid production and addresses the underlying hyperuricosuria and hyperuricemia. Calcium excretion and oxalate are within normal limits, making therapies targeting calcium oxalate stones unnecessary.

user: A 55-year-old male with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis three times weekly presents with progressive muscle weakness, ascending paralysis beginning in the lower extremities over 48 hours, and areflexia on examination. His most recent pre-dialysis labs (drawn 2 days after his last session due to a missed session) show serum potassium 7.2 mEq/L, sodium 138 mEq/L, bicarbonate 14 mEq/L, and BUN 98 mg/dL. ECG shows peaked T-waves in precordial leads, widened QRS complexes of 160 ms, and a sine wave pattern. Blood pressure is 158/94 mmHg. What is the sequence of immediate emergency interventions that must be administered?
ground_truth_answer: This patient has severe life-threatening hyperkalemia (K+ 7.2 mEq/L) with ECG changes indicating imminent cardiac arrest (peaked T-waves, wide QRS, sine wave pattern). The correct sequence of emergency interventions is: (1) Intravenous calcium gluconate (1–2 ampules of 10% calcium gluconate IV over 2–5 minutes) immediately to stabilize the cardiac membrane and counteract the cardiotoxic effects of hyperkalemia — this is the most urgent step and does not lower potassium but prevents arrhythmia; (2) Insulin (10 units regular insulin IV) with dextrose 50% (25–50 g IV) to shift potassium intracellularly, with onset in 15–30 minutes; (3) Sodium bicarbonate IV to correct acidosis and further shift K+ intracellularly; (4) Inhaled albuterol (10–20 mg nebulized) as adjunctive intracellular shift therapy; (5) Emergent hemodialysis, which is the definitive treatment to remove potassium from the body and is especially appropriate given this patient's ESRD and inability to excrete potassium renally. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) acts too slowly for this acute emergency and is not appropriate as an initial intervention.

user: A 28-year-old male presents with gross hematuria, hemoptysis, and dyspnea developing over 10 days. Serum creatinine has risen from 1.0 mg/dL to 4.7 mg/dL over this period. Urinalysis reveals 3+ blood, red blood cell casts, and 2+ proteinuria (quantified at 2.1 g/24 hours). Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Serologic workup shows: ANCA negative, ANA negative, anti-GBM antibody titer of 1:320 (strongly positive), complement C3 94 mg/dL (normal), C4 28 mg/dL (normal). Kidney biopsy reveals linear IgG staining along the glomerular basement membrane on immunofluorescence with >70% crescents on light microscopy. What is the diagnosis and what is the first-line treatment regimen?
ground_truth_answer: The diagnosis is anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease, also known as Goodpasture syndrome (given the concurrent pulmonary hemorrhage). The pathognomonic combination of rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with nephritic sediment (RBC casts), pulmonary hemorrhage, strongly positive anti-GBM antibodies at 1:320, and linear IgG deposition on glomerular basement membrane by immunofluorescence with crescentic nephritis (>50% crescents) confirms this diagnosis. Normal complement levels exclude lupus nephritis and MPGN. ANCA negativity excludes ANCA-associated vasculitis, though double-positivity (ANCA + anti-GBM) can occur but is not present here. The first-line treatment is triple therapy: (1) high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg/day for 3 days) followed by oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day; (2) cyclophosphamide (2 mg/kg/day orally) to suppress further antibody production; and (3) daily therapeutic plasmapheresis (plasma exchange of 4 liters per session, 14 sessions over 14 days) to rapidly clear circulating anti-GBM antibodies, which is critical particularly given the active pulmonary hemorrhage.

user: A 54-year-old woman with a known BRCA2 pathogenic variant is diagnosed with ER-positive, PR-positive, HER2-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (T2N1M0, Stage IIB). Oncotype DX recurrence score returns at 28. She has completed surgery and is being evaluated for adjuvant systemic therapy. Her oncologist notes she is premenopausal with adequate renal and hepatic function. Based on the TAILORx trial data and current NCCN guidelines, what is the most appropriate adjuvant systemic therapy recommendation?
ground_truth_answer: Adjuvant chemotherapy followed by endocrine therapy (ovarian suppression plus an aromatase inhibitor or tamoxifen). In premenopausal patients with node-positive, HR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer and an Oncotype DX score of 26 or higher, TAILORx and RxPONDER data support the addition of chemotherapy to endocrine therapy, as these patients derive significant benefit. Node-positive status (N1) independently supports chemotherapy regardless of recurrence score in premenopausal women per NCCN guidelines. Her BRCA2 status does not change this adjuvant recommendation but may prompt consideration of olaparib (per OlympiA trial criteria post-chemotherapy if high-risk criteria are met). The chemotherapy regimen would typically be anthracycline- and taxane-based.

user: A 67-year-old male with a 40 pack-year smoking history presents with a 3.2 cm right upper lobe lung mass. CT-guided biopsy confirms lung adenocarcinoma. Molecular profiling reveals an EGFR exon 20 insertion mutation (p.A763_Y764insFQEA). PET-CT shows no mediastinal involvement and no distant metastases (cT2aN0M0, Stage IB). The tumor board debates treatment. What is the appropriate first-line systemic treatment if this patient were to develop metastatic disease, and why does this differ from the management of classical EGFR-sensitizing mutations?
ground_truth_answer: For metastatic disease with an EGFR exon 20 insertion mutation, the appropriate first-line systemic therapy is amivantamab-vmjw plus carboplatin-pemetrexed chemotherapy, as approved by the FDA in 2024 based on the PAPILLON trial demonstrating superior progression-free survival over chemotherapy alone. This differs fundamentally from classical EGFR-sensitizing mutations (exon 19 deletions or L858R point mutations), which respond robustly to first-generation (erlotinib, gefitinib), second-generation (afatinib, dacomitinib), or third-generation (osimertinib) EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors. Exon 20 insertions cause steric alterations in the ATP-binding pocket that confer intrinsic resistance to standard EGFR TKIs by stabilizing the inactive kinase conformation. Mobocertinib is an alternative for previously platinum-treated patients but is not first-line. This distinction is critical because empiric EGFR TKI use in exon 20 insertion patients would result in treatment failure.

user: A 72-year-old male is diagnosed with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). Flow cytometry confirms CD5+/CD19+/CD23+/CD20dim phenotype. FISH cytogenetics reveals isolated deletion 17p13.1 (TP53 deletion) in 45% of cells. IGHV mutation status is unmutated. He is symptomatic with progressive lymphadenopathy, a lymphocyte count of 187,000/µL, and hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL meeting iwCLL criteria for treatment initiation. He has a creatinine clearance of 55 mL/min. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy and why is chemoimmunotherapy contraindicated?
ground_truth_answer: The most appropriate first-line therapy is a BTK inhibitor (acalabrutinib or ibrutinib) or venetoclax plus obinutuzumab. Chemoimmunotherapy (e.g., FCR — fludarabine, cyclophosphamide, rituximab) is contraindicated because del(17p)/TP53 deletion renders cells unable to undergo chemotherapy-induced p53-mediated apoptosis, resulting in primary resistance and markedly inferior outcomes with alkylating agents and purine analogs. Del(17p) is the single most important adverse prognostic marker in CLL for predicting chemoimmunotherapy failure. Acalabrutinib is preferred over ibrutinib in patients with cardiovascular comorbidities due to lower rates of atrial fibrillation. Venetoclax-obinutuzumab is a time-limited alternative with durable remissions. Unmutated IGHV and del(17p) together define an ultra-high-risk profile reinforcing the need for targeted therapy rather than chemotherapy.

user: A 45-year-old woman with metastatic melanoma (BRAF V600E-mutant, confirmed by PCR) achieves a complete radiographic response after 24 months of combined BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy with dabrafenib plus trametinib. She has no evidence of disease on PET-CT. She then develops symptomatic disease progression 8 months after stopping targeted therapy, with new hepatic metastases confirmed on biopsy. Repeat molecular profiling of the biopsy specimen shows retained BRAF V600E with an acquired MEK1 Q56P amplification. What is the most appropriate next systemic therapy?
ground_truth_answer: The most appropriate next systemic therapy is immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, specifically anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivelumab) or combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab, rather than re-challenging with BRAF/MEK inhibition. The acquired MEK1 Q56P amplification represents a MAP kinase pathway re-activation resistance mechanism that confers cross-resistance to standard dabrafenib/trametinib re-challenge. Although re-challenge with BRAF/MEK inhibitors after a treatment-free interval can sometimes yield responses when resistance is not fixed (e.g., due to clonal dynamics), the identification of a specific acquired resistance mutation (MEK1 amplification) on repeat biopsy indicates that re-challenge is unlikely to be effective. Immunotherapy is now the preferred strategy in this setting. Combination ipilimumab/nivolumab offers higher response rates in treatment-naive immunotherapy patients but with greater toxicity; anti-PD-1 monotherapy is an acceptable alternative depending on performance status and organ function.

user: A 38-year-old male presents with a painless right testicular mass. Orchiectomy is performed. Pathology reveals a pure seminoma, 4.8 cm in greatest dimension, with invasion of the rete testis but no lymphovascular invasion, no epididymal involvement, and no tunica vaginalis involvement. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows no retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (nodes all <1 cm) and no visceral metastases. Serum AFP is undetectable, beta-hCG is 3.1 mIU/mL (mildly elevated), and LDH is 1.1x upper limit of normal. Post-orchiectomy staging is pT2N0M0S1, Clinical Stage IS. What is the correct management, and why does the tumor size specifically alter risk stratification in this case?
ground_truth_answer: This patient has Clinical Stage IS seminoma (persistently elevated post-orchiectomy markers without radiographic metastatic disease), which mandates staging as Stage IS regardless of negative imaging. However, the critical distinction is that pure seminoma does not produce AFP; any AFP elevation in a supposed pure seminoma diagnosis mandates reclassification as a non-seminomatous germ cell tumor (NSGCT). Here AFP is undetectable and beta-hCG is only mildly elevated (which seminomas can produce), confirming pure seminoma. For Stage IS seminoma, BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) chemotherapy for 1-2 cycles is recommended because persistent marker elevation implies occult metastatic disease not detected on imaging, making surveillance or single-agent carboplatin inadequate. Regarding tumor size: for clinical Stage I seminoma, tumor size greater than 4 cm is a recognized risk factor for relapse (along with rete testis invasion) per the MRC/EORTC risk stratification model; this patient has both risk factors (4.8 cm and rete testis invasion), placing him in the high-risk Stage I category, which informs adjuvant therapy decisions if markers were normal — but given persistent marker elevation (Stage IS), chemotherapy is the definitive recommendation irrespective of size.

user: A 4-week-old male infant presents with projectile, non-bilious vomiting after every feeding for the past 5 days. He appears hungry immediately after vomiting. Physical exam reveals a palpable olive-shaped mass in the epigastrium. Labs show: serum Na+ 128 mEq/L, K+ 3.0 mEq/L, Cl- 82 mEq/L, HCO3- 34 mEq/L, arterial pH 7.54, urine Cl- < 10 mEq/L. What is the diagnosis and what specific metabolic derangement must be corrected before surgical intervention?
ground_truth_answer: The diagnosis is hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. The metabolic derangement is hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, evidenced by the elevated HCO3- (34 mEq/L), elevated pH (7.54), low Cl- (82 mEq/L), and low K+ (3.0 mEq/L). The low urine Cl- (<10 mEq/L) confirms chloride-avid state due to volume depletion. This must be corrected with IV isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl) with added KCl supplementation prior to pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt procedure), because proceeding to anesthesia during alkalosis risks apnea from blunted respiratory compensation and cardiac dysrhythmia. The alkalosis, not the pyloric obstruction itself, is the surgical contraindication.

user: A 3-year-old previously healthy girl presents with 3 days of periorbital edema, abdominal distension, and frothy urine. Urinalysis shows 4+ proteinuria, no hematuria, no RBC casts. Serum albumin is 1.4 g/dL, total cholesterol is 410 mg/dL, creatinine is 0.4 mg/dL (normal for age), and 24-hour urine protein is 78 mg/kg/day. Complement levels C3 and C4 are both normal. Renal biopsy is deferred. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the first-line treatment with its specific mechanism?
ground_truth_answer: The most likely diagnosis is minimal change disease (MCD), which is the cause of nephrotic syndrome in approximately 85-90% of children aged 1-8 years. The clinical picture — massive proteinuria (>40 mg/m²/hr or >3.5 g/day equivalent, here 78 mg/kg/day far exceeds the threshold of >40 mg/m²/hr), hypoalbuminemia (<2.5 g/dL), hyperlipidemia (cholesterol 410 mg/dL), edema, absence of hematuria, normal complement, and normal creatinine — is classic for MCD. First-line treatment is oral prednisone at 60 mg/m²/day (max 60 mg/day) for 4-6 weeks followed by 40 mg/m² alternate-day dosing for 4-6 weeks per ISKDC protocol. Prednisone acts by stabilizing the glomerular podocyte cytoskeleton and reducing T-lymphocyte-mediated permeability factor production, thereby restoring the glomerular filtration barrier and resolving proteinuria.

user: A 6-week-old male infant born at 38 weeks gestation is brought in for poor feeding and lethargy over 24 hours. He was well-appearing at his 2-week visit and received no newborn screening abnormality notifications. Temperature is 37.2°C, HR 155 bpm, RR 48/min. Serum glucose is 38 mg/dL. Plasma ammonia is 890 µmol/L (normal <50 µmol/L). Serum amino acids show markedly elevated citrulline at 1,240 µmol/L (normal <40 µmol/L). Urine orotic acid is elevated. Arterial blood gas shows pH 7.48, HCO3- 26 mEq/L (no primary acidosis). What is the precise enzymatic defect?
ground_truth_answer: The precise enzymatic defect is deficiency of argininosuccinate synthetase (ASS1), causing citrullinemia type I (classic citrullinemia). The diagnostic triad is: (1) severely elevated plasma citrulline (1,240 µmol/L; citrullinemia type I typically produces citrulline >1,000 µmol/L, distinguishing it from OTC deficiency where citrulline is low-normal, and from argininosuccinate lyase deficiency where argininosuccinate accumulates instead); (2) markedly elevated ammonia (890 µmol/L) reflecting urea cycle blockade; and (3) elevated urine orotic acid, which indicates overflow of carbamoyl phosphate into the pyrimidine synthesis pathway — a finding that rules out proximal defects such as CPS1 or NAGS deficiency where orotic acid is not elevated. The respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.48, normal HCO3-) reflects ammonia's direct stimulation of the respiratory center, a hallmark of urea cycle disorders rather than organic acidemias.

user: A 14-year-old female presents with recurrent episodes of syncope exclusively during vigorous exercise and one episode of syncope after a startling alarm clock. Resting ECG shows: heart rate 58 bpm, corrected QT interval (QTc) of 498 ms calculated by Bazett's formula, T-wave morphology showing late-onset broad bifid T waves in leads II, V4-V6, and no delta waves. Her mother had an ICD placed at age 22 for 'arrhythmia.' Genetic testing returns a missense mutation in KCNQ1. What is the specific subtype of this condition, and which trigger pattern pathophysiologically distinguishes it from the other common subtypes?
ground_truth_answer: The diagnosis is Long QT Syndrome Type 1 (LQT1), caused by loss-of-function mutations in KCNQ1 encoding the alpha subunit of the slow delayed rectifier potassium channel (IKs). The pathophysiologically distinguishing trigger is exercise/adrenergic stimulation: LQT1 events occur predominantly during sympathetic activation (swimming and exercise in 60-70% of events) because IKs current is critical for action potential shortening at fast heart rates under adrenergic tone — its absence causes exaggerated QT prolongation precisely when catecholamines surge. This contrasts mechanistically with LQT2 (KCNH2/IKr deficiency), which is triggered by auditory startle/emotional arousal at rest, and LQT3 (SCN5A gain-of-function), which causes events predominantly during sleep/bradycardia. Although this patient had one alarm-clock episode, KCNQ1 mutation genetically confirms LQT1 — alarm-clock response in LQT1 is documented because of its adrenergic component. The broad broad-based T-waves on ECG and QTc of 498 ms (≥460 ms threshold in females) are consistent with LQT1 morphology.

user: A 30-month-old male is evaluated for developmental regression over 4 months — he has lost previously acquired words, now has no purposeful hand use, and exhibits stereotyped hand-wringing movements. His head circumference curve shows normal growth to 6 months (38 cm) followed by deceleration to the 2nd percentile at 30 months (45 cm, expected ~49 cm). EEG shows multifocal spike-and-wave discharges. Genetic testing reveals a heterozygous nonsense mutation c.763C>T (p.Arg255X) in MECP2 on the X chromosome. What is the diagnosis, and why does this condition predominantly manifest clinically in females rather than males despite being X-linked?
ground_truth_answer: The diagnosis is Rett syndrome, caused by loss-of-function mutations in MECP2 (methyl-CpG binding protein 2) on Xq28. The condition predominantly manifests clinically in females rather than males due to the lethality of MECP2 loss in males: because MECP2 is X-linked and males are hemizygous (46,XY), a loss-of-function mutation leaves them with no functional MECP2, causing fatal neonatal encephalopathy or severe congenital hypotonia incompatible with survival to the age of clinical Rett syndrome presentation. Females are heterozygous (46,XX) and have random X-inactivation (lyonization), meaning approximately 50% of cells express the mutant allele and 50% express the wild-type allele — this cellular mosaicism is sufficient to sustain life and produces the characteristic clinical course with a period of apparently normal development followed by regression. The specific four-stage progression (normal development → rapid regression → plateau → late motor deterioration), acquired microcephaly, and hand-wringing stereotypies in a female with an MECP2 nonsense mutation are pathognomonic.

user: A 67-year-old male ex-smoker (45 pack-years, quit 3 years ago) undergoes low-dose CT screening and is found to have a solid pulmonary nodule measuring 8.2 mm in the right upper lobe. A prior CT from 22 months ago shows the same nodule measured 5.1 mm at that time. Using volumetric doubling time estimation (assuming spherical geometry), calculate the approximate volume doubling time and determine whether this growth pattern is consistent with a benign or malignant process according to Fleischner Society 2017 guidelines.
ground_truth_answer: Volume is proportional to the cube of diameter. At 5.1 mm, volume ∝ (5.1)^3 = 132.7 arbitrary units; at 8.2 mm, volume ∝ (8.2)^3 = 551.4 units. Volume doubling time (VDT) = 22 months × log(2) / log(551.4/132.7) = 22 × 0.693 / log(4.154) = 15.24 / 1.424 ≈ 10.7 months. A VDT between 400–600 days is considered indeterminate; VDT below 400 days (approximately 13.3 months) suggests malignancy, and VDT above 600 days suggests benignity. This nodule's VDT of approximately 320 days (10.7 months) falls below 400 days, indicating a growth rate highly suspicious for malignancy. Per Fleischner Society 2017 guidelines for solid nodules >6 mm in a high-risk patient, PET-CT or tissue sampling is recommended. The nodule should be classified as suspicious for malignancy based on its calculated VDT.

user: A 52-year-old nonsmoking female presents with progressive exertional dyspnea over 18 months. Pulmonary function testing reveals: FVC 58% predicted, FEV1 61% predicted, FEV1/FVC ratio 0.84, TLC 54% predicted, DLCO 38% predicted, and residual volume 62% predicted. High-resolution CT shows bilateral basal-predominant subpleural reticulation with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis, with no ground-glass opacity predominance. BAL differential shows 62% macrophages, 28% lymphocytes, 6% neutrophils, and 4% eosinophils. What is the most specific diagnosis and what single PFT finding most distinguishes this entity from hypersensitivity pneumonitis?
ground_truth_answer: The diagnosis is Usual Interstitial Pneumonia (UIP) pattern consistent with Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF). PFTs show a restrictive pattern (FVC, TLC, and RV all reduced with preserved or elevated FEV1/FVC ratio), and the severely reduced DLCO at 38% predicted reflects impaired gas exchange due to fibrosis and membrane thickening. The HRCT shows a classic UIP pattern: bilateral basal-predominant subpleural honeycombing with traction bronchiectasis, which confers a definite UIP pattern per ATS/ERS 2018 guidelines, making surgical lung biopsy unnecessary. The BAL lymphocytosis of 28% is notably below the threshold (>30%) typically associated with hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP); however, the single most distinguishing PFT finding from HP is that in IPF/UIP, TLC reduction is proportionally concordant with DLCO reduction without significant air trapping (RV/TLC ratio normal), whereas HP characteristically shows concurrent obstructive or mixed physiology with air trapping. The combination of definite UIP pattern on HRCT in a patient over 50 without identifiable cause establishes IPF as the diagnosis.

user: A 34-year-old male is admitted to the ICU following polytrauma. On day 3, he develops worsening hypoxemia: PaO2 52 mmHg on FiO2 0.80, PEEP 12 cmH2O. Chest X-ray shows bilateral infiltrates with no evidence of cardiac failure; echocardiogram confirms PCWP <18 mmHg. ABG: pH 7.31, PaCO2 51 mmHg, HCO3 25 mEq/L. The intensivist calculates his P/F ratio and oxygenation index (OI = MAP × FiO2 × 100 / PaO2) using a mean airway pressure (MAP) of 22 cmH2O. Based on the Berlin Definition of ARDS and the calculated OI, classify the severity of ARDS and determine whether the OI-based classification matches the P/F-based classification.
ground_truth_answer: P/F ratio = PaO2 / FiO2 = 52 / 0.80 = 65 mmHg. Per the Berlin Definition (2012), P/F ≤100 mmHg on PEEP ≥5 cmH2O classifies as Severe ARDS. Oxygenation Index = (MAP × FiO2 × 100) / PaO2 = (22 × 0.80 × 100) / 52 = 1760 / 52 ≈ 33.8. An OI ≥16 corresponds to Severe ARDS by validated OI-based ARDS severity thresholds (Mild: OI <8; Moderate: OI 8–16; Severe: OI >16). Both classification methods converge on Severe ARDS. The ABG also reveals a partially compensated respiratory acidosis (pH 7.31, PaCO2 51, HCO3 25), consistent with permissive hypercapnia under lung-protective ventilation. The patient meets all Berlin criteria: acute onset within 1 week of insult, bilateral opacities not explained by effusion or collapse, respiratory failure not explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (PCWP <18), and P/F ratio <100 on PEEP ≥5 cmH2O. The OI-based and P/F-based classifications are concordant at Severe ARDS.

user: A 48-year-old female with known systemic sclerosis presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion and a new dry cough. Resting room-air SpO2 is 96%, dropping to 88% on a 6-minute walk test. Right heart catheterization reveals: mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) 27 mmHg, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) 9 mmHg, cardiac output (Fick) 4.1 L/min. Calculate the pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) in Wood units and determine whether this patient meets the updated 2022 ESC/ERS hemodynamic definition of pulmonary arterial hypertension, and if so, which hemodynamic subtype.
ground_truth_answer: PVR = (mPAP - PCWP) / Cardiac Output = (27 - 9) / 4.1 = 18 / 4.1 ≈ 4.39 Wood units. Per the 2022 ESC/ERS Pulmonary Hypertension Guidelines, pulmonary hypertension is now defined as mPAP >20 mmHg (lowered from the previous threshold of 25 mmHg). This patient has mPAP of 27 mmHg, exceeding this threshold. Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) specifically requires: mPAP >20 mmHg, PCWP ≤15 mmHg (pre-capillary pattern), AND PVR >2 Wood units. This patient's PCWP is 9 mmHg (pre-capillary) and PVR is 4.39 Wood units (>2 WU), satisfying all three criteria. The hemodynamic subtype is pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension consistent with Group 1 PAH (pulmonary arterial hypertension) in the context of connective tissue disease (systemic sclerosis-associated PAH). This is a clinically critical distinction from post-capillary PH, which would require PCWP >15 mmHg and would mandate different management.

user: A 71-year-old male with a 60 pack-year smoking history and known COPD (FEV1 38% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.51, post-bronchodilator) presents to the emergency department with acute-on-chronic dyspnea. ABG on room air shows: pH 7.22, PaCO2 78 mmHg, PaO2 44 mmHg, HCO3 31 mEq/L. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation without focal consolidation. His serum electrolytes are normal, and his alveolar-arterial (A-a) gradient is calculated using the alveolar gas equation (barometric pressure 760 mmHg, FiO2 0.21, RQ 0.8). Determine whether this exacerbation is characterized by hypercapnic respiratory failure with an elevated or normal A-a gradient, and specify what this finding implies about the primary mechanism.
ground_truth_answer: Using the alveolar gas equation: PAO2 = (FiO2 × [PB - PH2O]) - (PaCO2 / RQ) = (0.21 × [760 - 47]) - (78 / 0.8) = (0.21 × 713) - 97.5 = 149.7 - 97.5 = 52.2 mmHg. A-a gradient = PAO2 - PaO2 = 52.2 - 44 = 8.2 mmHg. The normal A-a gradient for a 71-year-old is approximately 7–14 mmHg (using the formula: 2.5 + 0.21 × age = 2.5 + 14.9 ≈ 17.4 mmHg as upper limit; alternatively 4 + age/4 = 4 + 17.75 ≈ 21.75 mmHg). By the most conservative estimate, an A-a gradient of 8.2 mmHg is at the low end of normal or normal for this age. The ABG shows acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis: chronic metabolic compensation is evidenced by elevated HCO3 of 31 mEq/L (chronic CO2 retention), with an acute drop in pH to 7.22 indicating acute decompensation. A normal or near-normal A-a gradient in the setting of hypercapnic respiratory failure indicates that the primary mechanism is pure alveolar hypoventilation (reduced respiratory drive or muscular fatigue), rather than V/Q mismatch or diffusion impairment, which would produce a widened A-a gradient. This finding is characteristic of acute exacerbation of COPD precipitated by ventilatory muscle fatigue or central hypoventilation rather than a superimposed pneumonia or pulmonary embolism.

ground_truth_answer: Multiple equally valid interpretations exist. First, this could represent expressive language delay in isolation, which is common and often resolves spontaneously, making watchful waiting with speech-language referral appropriate. Second, it could represent a late-talking presentation within normal variation since some children reach two-word combinations between 24-30 months and this child has intact social communication skills. Third, a repeat audiologic evaluation is warranted regardless of diagnosis because newborn hearing screens do not exclude later-onset or progressive hearing loss, which could explain the expressive delay. Fourth, referral to speech-language pathology for formal evaluation is indicated by AAP guidelines at this milestone gap, independent of the underlying etiology. The preserved social pragmatics (eye contact, pointing, pretend play) argue against autism spectrum disorder but do not exclude it entirely without a structured assessment. No single diagnosis or next step is uniquely correct without formal speech-language and audiology evaluation.

user: During a 9-month well-child visit, a previously healthy infant is found to have a hemoglobin of 10.2 g/dL on a routine screen. The infant is exclusively breastfed, was born at 38 weeks gestation, and the family follows a vegetarian diet. The infant appears well, has normal growth, and the physical exam is unremarkable. What is causing the anemia and what should be done?
ground_truth_answer: At least three equally plausible etiologies exist and cannot be distinguished without further testing. Iron deficiency anemia is the most common cause at this age, particularly with exclusive breastfeeding beyond 6 months without iron supplementation and a vegetarian household diet reducing dietary iron exposure. However, late preterm birth at 38 weeks is associated with lower iron stores at birth compared to term infants, making this a contributing or primary factor. Vitamin B12 deficiency is a genuine concern given exclusive breastfeeding from a vegetarian mother, as maternal B12 deficiency depletes breast milk B12, and this can cause macrocytic or normocytic anemia at this age. Without a complete blood count with indices, reticulocyte count, serum ferritin, and B12 level, the specific etiology cannot be determined. Treatment with empiric iron alone could be appropriate for iron deficiency but would miss B12 deficiency, which carries risk of neurological injury if untreated. A confident single diagnosis without laboratory workup is not supportable.

user: A 7-year-old girl is referred for recurrent abdominal pain occurring 2-3 times per week for the past 3 months. Each episode lasts 30-60 minutes, is periumbilical, and is described as crampy. She misses school approximately once a week due to the pain. Her parents report episodes often occur on school mornings. Stool pattern is normal, there is no blood in stool, no weight loss, no fever, and growth is tracking at the 50th percentile. Physical exam is unremarkable with mild periumbilical tenderness on palpation.
ground_truth_answer: Multiple diagnoses are equally consistent with this clinical picture and cannot be prioritized without further evaluation. Functional abdominal pain or irritable bowel syndrome meeting Rome IV criteria is strongly supported by the chronicity, periumbilical location, normal growth, and absence of alarm features. Anxiety-related somatic pain is equally plausible given the temporal association with school mornings and school avoidance, but this label cannot be applied without psychosocial assessment since physical and psychological etiologies frequently coexist. Abdominal migraine is a recognized diagnosis in this age group presenting with episodic periumbilical cramping, though the absence of associated nausea, pallor, or headache history makes this less certain but not excludable. Constipation-related pain can present with normal stool frequency and should be excluded with history of stool consistency and possibly abdominal imaging. Celiac disease, lactose intolerance, and Helicobacter pylori infection can each produce this symptom pattern and require laboratory or breath testing to exclude. No single diagnosis is uniquely correct without further workup.

ground_truth_answer: This scenario is genuinely underdetermined and reasonable clinicians following different valid guidelines would reach different management decisions. With a modified McIsaac score of 2, guidelines differ: the IDSA recommends throat culture or rapid antigen detection test (RADT) to guide treatment rather than empiric antibiotics, making testing the appropriate next step. If a RADT is performed and positive, antibiotics are clearly indicated; if negative, the decision to confirm with culture versus discharge without treatment is itself contested, with some guidelines recommending culture confirmation in children due to RADT sensitivity of approximately 70-90%. Some practitioners following a test-and-treat strategy would not prescribe antibiotics at a score of 2 without a positive test result, while others in high-prevalence endemic settings or with limited access to rapid testing might apply a lower threshold. Additionally, the clinical picture is consistent with viral pharyngitis, infectious mononucleosis (which would contraindicate amoxicillin), or group A streptococcal pharyngitis, and these cannot be clinically distinguished without testing. The correct answer depends on whether testing is available, local prevalence, and which guideline framework is applied.

user: A 4-week-old male infant presents with non-bilious projectile vomiting after every feeding over the past 5 days. He is feeding eagerly and appears hungry immediately after vomiting. Birth weight was 3.4 kg and current weight is 3.2 kg. On exam he appears mildly dry with a sunken anterior fontanelle. The abdomen is soft and no olive-shaped mass is palpable on exam. What is the diagnosis and what should be done next?
ground_truth_answer: Although pyloric stenosis is the most clinically suspected diagnosis, this scenario is underdetermined because the pathognomonic olive-shaped mass is not palpable, which occurs in up to 40-60% of cases depending on examiner experience and infant cooperation. Multiple equally valid next steps and differential diagnoses must be considered. Pyloric stenosis remains strongly suspected and ultrasound is the definitive imaging modality of choice with sensitivity exceeding 95%, making ultrasound the next step most guidelines endorse. However, the same presentation is consistent with overfeeding, gastroesophageal reflux with forceful regurgitation mischaracterized as projectile, or formula intolerance, all of which can cause weight loss and dehydration in this age group. Upper GI series is an alternative diagnostic tool used in centers with less ultrasound expertise. The degree of dehydration described also requires assessment of electrolytes, as pyloric stenosis classically produces hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, and surgery is contraindicated until electrolytes are corrected, making laboratory evaluation a necessary concurrent step regardless of imaging findings. A confident diagnosis of pyloric stenosis without ultrasound confirmation is clinically inappropriate, and surgical referral alone without metabolic stabilization would be incorrect.

user: A 45-year-old nonsmoking woman with a BMI of 33 presents with a 3-month history of loud snoring reported by her partner, daytime sleepiness, and an Epworth Sleepiness Scale score of 11. Overnight pulse oximetry shows intermittent desaturations to 88%. What is the appropriate next diagnostic step?
ground_truth_answer: Multiple diagnostic approaches are equally guideline-supported. Full attended in-laboratory polysomnography (PSG) is the gold standard and is recommended by some societies (AASM) for any suspected sleep-disordered breathing. However, home sleep apnea testing (HSAT) with a Type III or IV device is also guideline-endorsed as an acceptable alternative for patients with high pretest probability of obstructive sleep apnea without significant comorbidities, and many payers require it first. Some practitioners would proceed directly to empirical auto-titrating CPAP (APAP) without formal testing given the high clinical probability, which is supported by certain clinical pathways. The choice between PSG, HSAT, or empirical APAP depends on institutional resources, payer policy, and interpretation of the patient's comorbidity burden—none of which are fully specified.

user: During a pre-operative evaluation, a chest X-ray of a 62-year-old male scheduled for elective knee replacement incidentally reveals a 7 mm solid pulmonary nodule in the right lower lobe. The patient has a 20 pack-year smoking history and quit 10 years ago. He is asymptomatic. What is the recommended follow-up plan?
ground_truth_answer: Multiple follow-up strategies are defensible depending on which guideline is applied. The Fleischner Society 2017 guidelines for incidentally detected nodules recommend CT follow-up at 6-12 months for a high-risk patient with a 6-8 mm nodule, with optional follow-up at 18-24 months. The ACR Lung-RADS system is designed for screening CT, not incidental findings, and may not apply here. Some clinicians would apply the British Thoracic Society (BTS) guidelines, which use a volume-doubling time approach and may recommend different intervals. Furthermore, classifying this patient as 'high risk' versus 'low risk' is ambiguous: a 20 pack-year history with 10 years cessation places him near the boundary of high-risk criteria used by different guidelines. The incidental nature versus screening context also affects which protocol should govern management, and no consensus mandates one over another.

user: A 55-year-old immunocompetent woman presents with a 6-week history of nonproductive cough, low-grade fever, and mild fatigue. A chest X-ray shows a unilateral patchy infiltrate in the right lower lobe. She has not been hospitalized recently and has no significant comorbidities. Her vital signs are stable and oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. What organism is most likely responsible?
ground_truth_answer: Multiple organisms are equally plausible causes of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in this clinical picture and cannot be distinguished without further testing. Mycoplasma pneumoniae is a leading candidate given the subacute course, nonproductive cough, and younger-to-middle-aged immunocompetent host. Chlamydophila pneumoniae presents identically. Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the most common bacterial cause of CAP overall and cannot be excluded based on the clinical description alone, though typical pneumococcal pneumonia is often more acute. Legionella pneumophila can present with a similar subacute syndrome, particularly with fatigue, and is not excluded without urinary antigen testing. Without sputum culture, serology, urinary antigen tests, or additional epidemiological history, assigning a single most likely organism is not justified and reflects a well-recognized limitation in clinical CAP management.

His BNP is 210 pg/mL and he reports no exertional symptoms, though he has reduced his activity level significantly over the past year. How should his aortic stenosis be classified and managed?
ground_truth_answer: This case is genuinely ambiguous across multiple dimensions. First, the stenosis severity itself is indeterminate: the aortic valve area of 1.05 cm² falls at the border between moderate (>1.0 cm²) and severe (<1.0 cm²) stenosis, and the low mean gradient of 28 mmHg is inconsistent with the near-severe valve area, raising the possibility of low-flow state, measurement error, or discordant grading. Second, the patient's symptomatic status is unclear: he denies exertional symptoms, but his significant activity reduction could represent either functional adaptation masking true symptoms (making him effectively 'symptomatic' by guideline definitions) or simply deconditioning from comorbidities. Third, management options are all defensible: watchful waiting with repeat imaging in 3–6 months, exercise stress testing to unmask symptoms, CT calcium scoring to adjudicate severity, or referral for valve intervention if symptoms are confirmed. ACC/AHA guidelines themselves acknowledge this gray zone and do not mandate a single pathway. A confident recommendation for or against intervention without further workup is not justified.

user: A 55-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history is found to have a new left bundle branch block (LBBB) on a pre-operative ECG before elective knee replacement surgery. Her resting heart rate is 72 bpm, blood pressure is 138/84 mmHg, and she reports mild dyspnea on climbing two flights of stairs that she attributes to deconditioning. Her NT-proBNP is 380 pg/mL. What is the significance of this finding and what should be done before proceeding with surgery?
ground_truth_answer: This scenario admits multiple equally valid interpretations and management strategies. Regarding the LBBB itself: it may represent new-onset LBBB secondary to ischemic cardiomyopathy (requiring urgent evaluation), rate-related LBBB (though rate is normal here), or idiopathic conduction system disease of no immediate hemodynamic consequence. The dyspnea is ambiguous: it may represent heart failure, reduced ejection fraction masked by LBBB, deconditioning, or pulmonary disease. The NT-proBNP of 380 pg/mL is elevated but non-specific in a 55-year-old woman and overlaps with both normal aging, early heart failure, and non-cardiac causes. Management options with guideline support include: (1) proceeding with echocardiography to assess LV function before any further decision, (2) cardiology referral and stress testing to exclude ischemia, (3) postponing elective surgery pending full cardiac workup per ACC perioperative guidelines, or (4) proceeding with surgery given the low-risk nature of the procedure with enhanced monitoring. No single pathway is uniquely mandated without additional information about LV function and ischemic burden.
